section.title: Sennacherib Threatens Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: This narrative opens the Hezekiah interlude, chapters 36 to 39, a historical pause between the oracles of judgment and the Book of Comfort. Assyria's field commander mocks Judah's trust in Egypt and in the LORD alike, but Hezekiah takes the blasphemous letter straight to the temple and to prayer, and the LORD alone, without a single Judean sword raised, destroys the army besieging His city and strikes down the boastful king himself.
section.thesis: When Assyria's spokesman taunts Jerusalem with the question 'How can the LORD deliver you?', King Hezekiah answers not with defiance or despair but with prayer, and the LORD vindicates His own name before all the kingdoms of the earth by destroying the Assyrian army untouched by human hands, for the sake of His own glory and His promise to David.
section.frameNote: Six scenes: the Rabshakeh's public threat outside Jerusalem's walls; his psychological warfare aimed at the people on the wall; Hezekiah's mourning and Isaiah's first word of assurance; Sennacherib's letter and Hezekiah's prayer in the temple; the LORD's answer through Isaiah, taunting the king of Assyria in turn; and the angel of the LORD striking down the Assyrian camp.

units.label: The Rabshakeh's public threat
units.note: trusting in Egypt, trusting in the LORD

verses.text: In the fourteenth year of Hezekiah's reign, Sennacherib king of Assyria attacked and captured all the fortified cities of Judah.
And the king of Assyria sent the Rabshakeh, with a great army, from Lachish to King Hezekiah at Jerusalem. And he stopped by the aqueduct of the upper pool, on the road to the Launderer's Field.
Then Eliakim son of Hilkiah the palace administrator, Shebna the scribe, and Joah son of Asaph the recorder, went out to him.
The Rabshakeh said to them, “Tell Hezekiah that this is what the great king, the king of Assyria, says: What is the basis of this confidence of yours?
You claim to have a strategy and strength for war, but these are empty words. In whom are you now trusting, that you have rebelled against me?
Look now, you are trusting in Egypt, that splintered reed of a staff that will pierce the hand of anyone who leans on it. Such is Pharaoh king of Egypt to all who trust in him.

But if you say to me, ‘We {{batach|trust}} in the LORD our God,’ is He not the One whose high places and altars Hezekiah has removed, saying to Judah and Jerusalem, ‘You must worship before this altar’?
Now, therefore, make a bargain with my master, the king of Assyria. I will give you two thousand horses, if you can put riders on them!
For how can you repel a single officer among the least of my master's servants when you depend on Egypt for chariots and horsemen?
So now, was it apart from the LORD that I have come up against this land to destroy it? The LORD Himself said to me, ‘Go up against this land and destroy it.’”

units.label: Psychological warfare on the wall
units.note: do not let Hezekiah deceive you

verses.text: Then Eliakim, Shebna, and Joah said to the Rabshakeh, “Please speak to your servants in Aramaic, since we understand it. Do not speak to us in Hebrew in the hearing of the people on the wall.”

But the Rabshakeh replied, “Has my master sent me to speak these words only to you and your master, and not to the men sitting on the wall, who are destined with you to eat their own dung and drink their own urine?”
Then the Rabshakeh stood and called out loudly in Hebrew: “Hear the words of the great king, the king of Assyria!
This is what the king says: Do not let Hezekiah deceive you, for he cannot deliver you.
Do not let Hezekiah persuade you to trust in the LORD when he says, ‘The LORD will surely deliver us; this city will not be given into the hand of the king of Assyria.’

Do not listen to Hezekiah, for this is what the king of Assyria says: Make peace with me and come out to me. Then every one of you will eat from his own vine and his own fig tree, and drink water from his own cistern,
until I come and take you away to a land like your own, a land of grain and new wine, a land of bread and vineyards.
Do not let Hezekiah mislead you when he says, ‘The LORD will deliver us.’ Has the god of any nation ever delivered his land from the hand of the king of Assyria?
Where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim? Have they delivered Samaria from my hand?
Who among all the gods of these lands has delivered his land from my hand? How then can the LORD deliver Jerusalem from my hand?”
But the people remained silent and did not answer a word, for Hezekiah had commanded, “Do not answer him.”
Then Hilkiah's son Eliakim the palace administrator, Shebna the scribe, and Asaph's son Joah the recorder came to Hezekiah with their clothes torn, and they relayed to him the words of the Rabshakeh.

units.label: Hezekiah's mourning and Isaiah's word
units.note: lift up a prayer for the remnant

verses.text: On hearing this report, King Hezekiah tore his clothes, put on sackcloth, and entered the house of the LORD.
And he sent Eliakim the palace administrator, Shebna the scribe, and the leading priests, all wearing sackcloth, to the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz
to tell him, “This is what Hezekiah says: Today is a day of distress, rebuke, and disgrace; for children have come to the point of birth, but there is no strength to deliver them.

Perhaps the LORD your God will hear the words of the Rabshakeh, whom his master the king of Assyria has sent to {{charaph|defy}} the living God, and He will rebuke him for the words that the LORD your God has heard. Therefore lift up a prayer for the remnant that still survives.”
So the servants of King Hezekiah went to Isaiah,
who replied, “Tell your master that this is what the LORD says: ‘Do not be afraid of the words you have heard, with which the servants of the king of Assyria have blasphemed Me.
Behold, I will put a spirit in him so that he will hear a rumor and return to his own land, where I will cause him to fall by the sword.’”
When the Rabshakeh heard that the king of Assyria had left Lachish, he withdrew and found the king fighting against Libnah.
Now Sennacherib had been warned about Tirhakah king of Cush: “He has set out to fight against you.” On hearing this, Sennacherib sent messengers to Hezekiah, saying,
“Give this message to Hezekiah king of Judah: ‘Do not let your God, in whom you trust, deceive you by saying that Jerusalem will not be delivered into the hand of the king of Assyria.
Surely you have heard what the kings of Assyria have done to all the other countries, devoting them to destruction. Will you then be spared?
Did the gods of the nations destroyed by my fathers rescue those nations, the gods of Gozan, Haran, and Rezeph, and of the people of Eden in Telassar?
Where are the kings of Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivvah?’”
So Hezekiah received the letter from the messengers, read it, and went up to the house of the LORD and spread it out before the LORD.
And Hezekiah prayed to the LORD:
“O LORD of Hosts, God of Israel, enthroned above the cherubim, You alone are God over all the kingdoms of the earth. You made the heavens and the earth.
Incline Your ear, O LORD, and hear; open Your eyes, O LORD, and see. Listen to all the words that Sennacherib has sent to defy the living God.
Truly, O LORD, the kings of Assyria have laid waste all these countries and their lands.
They have cast their gods into the fire and destroyed them, for they were not gods, but only wood and stone, the work of human hands.
And now, O LORD our God, save us from his hand, so that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that You alone, O LORD, are God.”

units.label: The LORD's answer through Isaiah
units.note: the zeal of the LORD will accomplish this

verses.text: Then Isaiah son of Amoz sent a message to Hezekiah: “This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: Because you have prayed to Me concerning Sennacherib king of Assyria,
this is the word that the LORD has spoken against him: ‘The Virgin Daughter of Zion despises you and mocks you; the Daughter of Jerusalem shakes her head behind you.
Whom have you taunted and {{charaph|blasphemed}}? Against whom have you raised your voice and lifted your eyes in pride? Against the Holy One of Israel!

Through your servants you have taunted the Lord, and you have said: “With my many chariots I have ascended to the heights of the mountains, to the remote peaks of Lebanon. I have cut down its tallest cedars, the finest of its cypresses. I have reached its farthest heights, the densest of its forests.
I have dug wells and drunk foreign waters. With the soles of my feet I have dried up all the streams of Egypt.”
Have you not heard? Long ago I ordained it; in days of old I planned it. Now I have brought it to pass, that you should crush fortified cities into piles of rubble.
Therefore their inhabitants, devoid of power, are dismayed and ashamed. They are like plants in the field, tender green shoots, grass on the rooftops, scorched before it is grown.
But I know your sitting down, your going out and coming in, and your raging against Me.
Because your rage and arrogance against Me have reached My ears, I will put My hook in your nose and My bit in your mouth; I will send you back the way you came.’

And this will be a sign to you, O Hezekiah: This year you will eat what grows on its own, and in the second year what springs from the same. But in the third year you will sow and reap; you will plant vineyards and eat their fruit.
And the surviving remnant of the house of Judah will again take root below and bear fruit above.
For a remnant will go forth from Jerusalem, and survivors from Mount Zion. The zeal of the LORD of Hosts will accomplish this.
So this is what the LORD says about the king of Assyria: ‘He will not enter this city or shoot an arrow into it. He will not come before it with a shield or build up a siege ramp against it.
He will go back the way he came, and he will not enter this city,’ declares the LORD.
‘I will defend this city and save it for My own sake and for the sake of My servant David.’”

units.label: The angel of the LORD strikes

verses.text: Then the angel of the LORD went out and struck down 185,000 men in the camp of the Assyrians. When the people got up the next morning, there were all the dead bodies!
So Sennacherib king of Assyria broke camp and withdrew. He returned to Nineveh and stayed there.

One day, while he was worshiping in the temple of his god Nisroch, his sons Adrammelech and Sharezer put him to the sword and escaped to the land of Ararat. And his son Esar-haddon reigned in his place.

batach.gloss: To trust, to lean one's full weight on someone or something with confidence. The Rabshakeh mocks Judah's trust in Egypt as leaning on a splintered reed, then twists the very word Judah would use of the LORD, exposing that the real question of the whole narrative is not military strategy but where trust ultimately rests.

charaph.gloss: To defy, taunt, or blaspheme, to hurl scorn at someone as though they were beneath notice. Sennacherib's servants use this word against the living God Himself, and the LORD's answering oracle in chapter 37 throws the same word back at the king, promising that whoever taunts the Holy One of Israel will himself be brought low.

basic.context: Assyria's king Sennacherib has swept through Judah's fortified cities and now sends his field commander, the Rabshakeh, to demand Jerusalem's surrender. In blistering public speech he mocks Judah's trust in Egypt and in the LORD alike, asking how any god could deliver a city from Assyria's hand. King Hezekiah, rather than answering in kind, tears his clothes, goes to the temple, and sends for the prophet Isaiah, and eventually spreads the enemy's own letter out before the LORD in prayer.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real historical event, corroborated in outline by 2 Kings 18 to 19, 2 Chronicles 32, and Assyrian records that, tellingly, never claim to have captured Jerusalem itself. The narrative's theological center is not military strategy but the question the Rabshakeh keeps raising, on whom does Judah's trust really rest, and the LORD answers by acting entirely without human military help, striking down the Assyrian army and vindicating His own name.
basic.originalLanguages: The Rabshakeh's whole speech circles the word {{batach}}, trust, mocking Egypt as a splintered reed and twisting even the language of trusting the LORD into an accusation. His masters' messengers go further, using the word {{charaph}}, to defy or blaspheme, against the living God Himself, language the LORD's own answering oracle in chapter 37 turns directly back upon the king of Assyria.
basic.typology: Hezekiah's response, taking a blasphemous threat and laying it open before God in prayer rather than answering in his own strength, anticipates the pattern of every believer's access to God through intercession, and points toward Christ, the greater Son of David for whose sake, alongside His own name, the LORD says He will defend the city, verse 35. The angel of the LORD striking down an army without a human hand raised anticipates the pattern of salvation that is God's work alone, not human achievement.
basic.christ: The LORD explicitly says He will save Jerusalem 'for My own sake and for the sake of My servant David,' verse 35, tying this deliverance to the Davidic covenant that runs forward to Christ, the Son of David, Matthew 1:1. Hezekiah's prayer, taking the enemy's taunting letter and laying it before God rather than trusting his own resources, models the access to the Father that believers now have boldly through Christ, our great High Priest, Hebrews 4:14-16.
basic.god: God alone delivers Jerusalem, without so much as an arrow shot by Judah, so that, as Hezekiah prays, all the kingdoms of the earth may know that the LORD alone is God. He hears and answers prayer even when His own name has been openly blasphemed, and He acts both for His own glory and out of covenant faithfulness to His promise to David.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 18:13-19:37 and 2 Chronicles 32:1-23 record the same events. Psalm 46 is traditionally associated with this deliverance, celebrating God as a very present help in trouble. Hebrews 4:14-16 invites believers to approach God's throne boldly, as Hezekiah brought his crisis directly to the LORD. Matthew 1:1 traces Christ's lineage to David, for whose sake the LORD defends Jerusalem here.
basic.oneStory: This narrative shows a king who, when mocked and threatened beyond his own strength, brings the crisis to God in prayer rather than trusting alliances or despair, and a God who answers by acting alone, for His own name and for the sake of His covenant with David. It is a preview of the greater deliverance God works through David's greatest Son, Christ, who saves His people not by their strength but by His own.

context.literary: A tightly constructed historical narrative in six scenes, moving from public threat, through psychological pressure on the people, to the king's private grief and prayer, to the LORD's own oracle of judgment against the boastful king, and closing with the deliverance itself and the tyrant's downfall.
context.historical: Set in 701 BC, the fourteenth year of Hezekiah, during Sennacherib's campaign against Judah after Hezekiah had rebelled against Assyrian vassalage; the same events are also narrated in 2 Kings 18 to 19 and 2 Chronicles 32, and Sennacherib's own annals confirm he besieged Jerusalem but, notably, never claim to have captured it.
context.canonical: Opens the Hezekiah narrative interlude, chapters 36 to 39, a historical pause between the woes and comfort chapters of Isaiah that also functions almost identically to 2 Kings 18 to 20, showing how deeply this deliverance shaped Israel's memory of the LORD as its defender.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Jerusalem's survival against an overwhelming empire came not from military strength or clever diplomacy but from the LORD's own action in response to a king who brought his crisis to God in humble, believing prayer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a specific historical deliverance; it is not a formula guaranteeing that every prayer for national rescue will be answered in the same visible way. Its abiding principle is that God hears prayer offered in genuine trust, especially when His own name and honor are at stake, and that ultimate security rests in Him rather than in human alliances.

debates.question: How did the LORD strike down the 185,000 Assyrian troops, by a direct, unmediated act, or through some natural means such as plague, sent providentially?

views.label: A direct, unmediated act of judgment
views.case: The text simply says the angel of the LORD struck them down, without naming any secondary cause, so the most natural reading is a direct supernatural act of judgment.

views.label: A providential act through a natural instrument
views.case: Ancient historians such as Herodotus preserve traditions of plague striking Assyrian forces in Egypt around this period, and Scripture elsewhere attributes plagues and natural disasters fully to the LORD's own hand even when a means is involved.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text credits the deliverance entirely to the LORD's own action rather than to Judah's military strength, which is the narrative's central theological point regardless of the precise mechanism.

study.originalLanguages: The Rabshakeh's speech turns again and again on {{batach}}, trust, mocking Egypt as a broken reed and even twisting Judah's trust in the LORD into an accusation of rebellion. His masters go further still, using {{charaph}}, to defy or blaspheme, against the living God, the very word the LORD's own oracle in chapter 37 turns back upon Sennacherib himself.

typology.type: Hezekiah spreading the enemy's letter before the LORD in prayer
typology.fulfillment: The believer's bold access to God's throne of grace in every crisis, secured through Christ, Hebrews 4:14-16.

typology.type: The LORD defending Jerusalem 'for the sake of My servant David'
typology.fulfillment: God's covenant faithfulness fulfilled and completed in Christ, the Son of David, Matthew 1:1, Luke 1:32-33.

study.christ: The LORD ties this deliverance explicitly to His covenant with David, saving Jerusalem 'for My own sake and for the sake of My servant David,' verse 35, the same covenant that runs forward to Christ, great David's greater Son, in whom every promise to David finds its lasting fulfillment, Luke 1:32-33. Hezekiah's example, laying an overwhelming threat before God in prayer rather than trusting his own resources, models the access to the Father that Christ now secures for all who come to Him, Hebrews 4:14-16.
study.god: God alone accomplishes this deliverance, without Judah lifting a weapon, so that, exactly as Hezekiah prayed, all the kingdoms of the earth may know that the LORD alone is God. He hears prayer even in the face of open blasphemy against His name, and He acts both to vindicate His own glory and to keep faith with His covenant promise to David.

crossReferences.note: The parallel historical account of the same crisis and deliverance, told with some additional narrative detail.
The Chronicler's account of the same events, emphasizing Hezekiah's leadership and preparations alongside his prayer.
Traditionally associated with this deliverance, celebrating God as a very present help in trouble who breaks the weapons of the nations.
Invites believers to approach God's throne of grace boldly, echoing Hezekiah's bold laying of his crisis before the LORD.

study.oneStory: This narrative shows a king who, facing a threat far beyond his strength and an enemy who openly blasphemed the living God, brought the crisis directly to the LORD in prayer, and a God who answered by acting alone, for His own name and in faithfulness to His covenant with David. It previews the greater deliverance God works through David's greatest Son, Christ, who saves His people not by their own strength but entirely by His.

quiz.q: What is the central taunt the Rabshakeh presses on Judah in chapter 36?

options.text: That their trust, whether in Egypt or in the LORD, is misplaced and cannot save them from Assyria.
options.note: The Rabshakeh's speech repeatedly circles the question of trust, mocking both Egypt and the LORD as unable to deliver.

options.text: That Judah's army is too large for Assyria to defeat honestly.
options.note: The opposite; the Rabshakeh mocks Judah's military weakness, not its strength.

options.text: That Hezekiah's religious reforms were too generous to foreigners.
options.note: Not the substance of the taunt, which concerns misplaced trust and Assyria's overwhelming power, not foreign policy toward outsiders.

options.text: That Assyria secretly fears Jerusalem's defenses.
options.note: The opposite; the Rabshakeh presents Assyria as supremely confident and Jerusalem as helpless.

quiz.q: How does Hezekiah respond upon hearing the Rabshakeh's report in 37:1?

options.text: He tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth, and enters the house of the LORD.
options.note: Verse 1 describes exactly this response of grief and prayer rather than panic or retaliation.

options.text: He immediately surrenders the city to avoid bloodshed.
options.note: The opposite; Hezekiah turns to prayer and seeks the LORD's help, not surrender.

options.text: He orders the immediate execution of the Assyrian messengers.
options.note: Not Hezekiah's response; he commands silence and then turns to prayer.

options.text: He seeks a new military alliance with a foreign power.
options.note: The opposite of his response here; he seeks the LORD directly rather than another human alliance.

quiz.q: What does Hezekiah do with Sennacherib's threatening letter in 37:14?

options.text: He spreads it out before the LORD in the temple and prays.
options.note: Verse 14 describes exactly this act of bringing the letter directly before God in prayer.

options.text: He burns the letter without reading it.
options.note: The opposite; he reads it and then brings it before the LORD rather than destroying it unread.

options.text: He sends a written reply agreeing to Assyria's terms.
options.note: Not what happens; Hezekiah seeks the LORD rather than negotiating surrender.

options.text: He hides the letter from his officials and the prophet Isaiah.
options.note: The opposite; the crisis is shared with Isaiah, and the letter is brought openly before the LORD.

quiz.q: For whose sake does the LORD say He will defend Jerusalem in 37:35?

options.text: For His own sake and for the sake of His servant David.
options.note: Verse 35 states this explicitly, tying the deliverance to God's glory and His covenant with David.

options.text: For the sake of Judah's military achievements.
options.note: The opposite; the deliverance is credited entirely to the LORD, not to Judah's own military accomplishments.

options.text: For the sake of a treaty Hezekiah made with Egypt.
options.note: The chapter mocks reliance on Egypt rather than crediting it as the basis for deliverance.

options.text: For the sake of the wealth Hezekiah had accumulated.
options.note: Not the stated reason; the LORD names His own name and His covenant with David, not Hezekiah's wealth.

quiz.q: How does the LORD deliver Jerusalem from the Assyrian army in 37:36?

options.text: The angel of the LORD strikes down 185,000 men in the Assyrian camp in a single night.
options.note: Verse 36 describes exactly this act of direct divine deliverance without human military engagement.

options.text: Judah's army defeats Assyria in open battle.
options.note: The opposite; the text emphasizes that Judah did not need to fight, since the LORD acted alone.

options.text: Egypt's army arrives to rescue Jerusalem.
options.note: Egypt's help was mocked earlier as unreliable; the deliverance comes from the LORD alone, not Egypt.

options.text: Sennacherib voluntarily withdraws out of respect for Hezekiah.
options.note: The withdrawal follows the LORD's direct act of judgment on the army, not a voluntary change of heart.

questions: The Rabshakeh's central question was, on whom does your trust really rest? Where is that same question being asked of you right now, in a threat, a diagnosis, or a financial pressure?

Hezekiah took the enemy's blasphemous letter and spread it out before the LORD rather than trying to answer it in his own strength. What are you carrying today that you need to lay before God rather than manage alone?
The people were commanded to remain silent rather than answer the Rabshakeh's taunts. Where might silence and prayer serve you better than an immediate defensive answer?

The LORD acted alone, without a single Judean weapon raised, so that all the kingdoms of the earth would know He alone is God. Where in your life might God be asking you to step back and let Him act rather than striving in your own strength?

God delivered Jerusalem for His own name and for the sake of His covenant with David, a promise that runs forward to Christ. How does knowing God keeps His covenant promises across centuries strengthen your confidence in the promises He has made to you in Christ?

translationNotes.note: Rabshakeh is a title, chief cupbearer or field commander, not a personal name, referring to a senior Assyrian officer sent to deliver Sennacherib's ultimatum.

The murder of Sennacherib by his own sons is independently confirmed in Babylonian records, occurring roughly twenty years after this siege, showing the LORD's word of judgment against him was fulfilled in full.

The number 185,000 is given without further explanation of the means; ancient historians such as Herodotus preserve independent traditions of a disaster striking Assyrian forces in this campaign, though Scripture attributes the act directly to the angel of the LORD.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 36:1-37:38. No em or en dashes appeared in the source text for this passage.
